speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Into-- into family, yeah. Which probably later on would be the ghetto in Poland. Now, this family were two adults with two small daughters. And my mother had a job. Whether it was charity or whether it was-- what it was, I don't know. But she was actually in the morning getting up and taking bread to people, loaves of bread. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she get the bread from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that's-- I don't know. But probably it was baked maybe clandestinely for the black market, and the-- she-- she made-- she had some rich people where she had to deliver it. And whatever-- I don't know what she got in money. But whatever it was, we had plenty of bread. Bread. And the people where we lived had, I think, a shop. But we never found, really. We found out what they did, but I think it's-- it's dairy products. And from there, we got milk. Bread and milk, bread and milk, bread and milk. For weeks, nothing else. Now, we had one room. And we all sat there during the day, because they were out. We had the run of the kitchen. And in the kitchen, there was a little-- what is called. What do they call these, this way, a walk in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not call it pantry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep the food in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it to keep the food in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. There's another name for it. I probably find out later. But never mind. The pantry. Call it pantry. You could walk in. And there on shelves-- there were empty shelves, but two plates. On each plate was about a quarter of a pound of butter on one, and a quarter of a pound of cheese on the other. Well, it wasn't ours. It was to look at, but not to eat. But I visited this pantry quite often. And look, but didn't touch. But one day, I found that the top of the butter and the top of the cheese had like a design on-- on them. I was wondering. I said, ah. Somebody wants to find out if it had been touched. And that was too-- too much of a challenge-- challenge for me. I got myself a knife, lifted the butter, and cut about a millimeter off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I had no bread at the time. Should I give it to Mother. Share it with sister. What will they say? I mean, it-- it wasn't done, such a thing. So who can remember the taste of butter after so many years? Well, I did-- I can. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, I sort of found that, you know, I-- I change it, sometimes to-- same way, bit of cheese. And sometimes a bit of butter. And I didn't have a bad conscience because every time something was finished, it was immediately renewed. So they didn't suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- was your father still in prison during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was still in prison. Now, the most peculiar thing of all which happened, you know, in-- in all these years, I cannot remember the moment, which must have been a very big moment, when Father joined us again. He was there. Because I remember him being with us, and with his ingenuity we had to eat. And during the time-- oh, yes. We-- we sold-- before he-- before he came out, we sold the first thing. And what my mother decided, of course, was my-- an old winter coat, my father's winter coat. And we had the-- the food which we brought-- brought to my father daily had of stopped, and he must have realized we had-- we were on bad times. But suddenly, he got food again because we sold the coat. We had-- you know, it was, of course, a little bit of variety. Whatever there was to be had. And my mother could cook something. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the time was when they were telling us that there would be a ghetto soon. My father had been out for a long time. And for what reason ever, we went to Bochnia, a town not far from Krakow. Quite a few miles, but not very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you move there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was '41 already. And there we came to a family, and the name only-- at first, when I first wrote my script to my book, I didn't know the name until I found somebody who knew the family only almost recently. The name is Fogelhut. Now, whereas in my German translation I use the name in this book, I don't know if I managed to put it-- found it in time to put it in my English version. Anyway, Fogelhut. Because-- I name names because in the hope that if anybody reads my book or sees the film, would come across anybody who knows these names. And perhaps in my lifetime will come back to me and say oh, yes, I know these people. And in some cases, we had to be-- have to give my belated thanks for things, and in other cases I could say, well, I have a word to speak with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But between going to Poland-- or, sorry. Between the beginning of the war until you moved to Bochnia, what was going on sort of with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the Germans sort of, you know, you could-- you could hear very great anti-Semitic incidents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But not actual mass killings of any sort, Hear of atrocities when they caught religious Jews and they cut their beards and other things, what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was going on where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was going on during the sort of, you know, whatever-- so, you know, telling that up to the point we went into Bochnia. And Bochnia, a small town, for some reason we had more to eat. Now, we had one room about-- it's in my book, I think. 4 by-- 3 and 1/2 by 6 meters, roughly. And this was in the small room. We lived there, and we had to wash there. We had corners. And we had a corner where there was a stove which was for warmth and for cooking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day, we had an arrival. However he came across to us, I don't know. My grandfather arrived. My grandfather from Germany, actually. He was also in the tunnel, and he managed to stay-- to go back to Germany. Anyway, he arrived. How, I don't know. But soon, our joy turned into something less than that because he was 10 years a-- a widower, and he found a woman. And he married her by the-- by the age of 67. And she moved in with us. A double wardrobe the size-- the width of a bed. We turned sideways, behind which they had their bed. We moved into another corner. I slept in a mattress on the floor. My-- my sister, little sister, slept with the parents. So now we were six in that room. Now, this good lady, we would have-- a very nice lady, would have congratulated him to his good taste and good fortune after being 10 years a widower. But this good lady had a-- a brother. And my grandfather brought along some money as well. But it soon run out. And we caught the lady taking some bread, hiding it and taking it to her brother who had wife and children. Well, under normal circumstances, this would be very charitable for-- for her, and for us to look away, or even give it to her. But these were not the times. We were hungry ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, as luck would have it, perhaps you can call it that way, he moved away soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found-- no, the grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: With her. Found another place. And just before he left, he told us that he had an arrangement with somebody in Krakow the name of Urbach who would give him money. And after the wall, should he survive, he would be paid in dollars by an aunt in America. When his money run out, we had a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: We could get more money, in-- in Krakow, but who would make-- would make the journey? Now, the-- I-- I'm sorry. I just saw somebody outside we want to come in. Is it-- can we stop that for a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now back to that incident. Well, I was then chosen to go with my sister. We, of course, didn't wear the arm bands. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point it must have been '41, '42. So I would-- was 18, 19. 18, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or was six years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was quite young to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --going with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the least Jewish-looking of us all. But as I say, she was a bit young. And-- but she-- when I was-- tried to chicken out, you know, when we went to the station to get our tickets to Krakow. And I wanted to chicken out, and she had sort of like an innocent smile of there was nothing to worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, you, my big brother, you know. It's not like you. Well, how could I sort of chicken out when my sister was so brave? Anyway, we went. We stepped into the train. And I pulled my cap over my eyes and pretended to sleep. And-- and the conductor came. She had the tickets. So we went to Krakow. And we got quite a sum of money. And wherever we went on the way back, we saw people-- I mean, in our imagination, staring at us. And who knows whether that's true or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one time even hiding in a doorway until a certain person passed by. These-- these are little sort of adventures only to show the fear. Anyway, we came back home and to the relief of our parents, we had-- we were absolutely OK. And we also had the money, which we then this-- at this time shared with the grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all in zloty. Yeah. Now I have to explain-- explain who this-- this Mr. Urbach was in friend-- in-- in Krakow. In Krakow, he had a electric shop. And since Jews couldn't have had-- could not-- were not allowed to have any shops anymore, there was a so-called Volksdeutsche, or ethnic German, who took it over. [GERMAN], in German. As a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- he-- but he said-- he claimed he couldn't run the shop without him. So he became his manager. And that's how he made still quite a lot of money, especially in-- in wartime. Everything, there was not-- no goods manufactured anymore. Or if-- if there were, it was only sold in the black market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, that's how he had the money. And he managed to survive and ask my aunt in America after the war, he asked for the money. And she asked me how much. And I explained to her, well, it depends how you look at it. At that time, the bank paid 35 zloty for the dollar. And on the black market, it was 175 zloty for the dollar. And well, you work it out with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, back to the little flat, or rather the room in Bochnia. Was your journey back uneventful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, except for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Being embarrassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --imaginary-- no, no. In the way back, imaginary sort of--

speaker: interviewer
text: That people were looking at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. Yeah. Dangers. I wish all the other dangers to come would have been imaginary. Now, that's when we had enough money for a while. And that's where grandmother-- grandfather and wife moved out. And that is when already the borders of Bochnia were defined, and we were suddenly inside a ghetto. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the borders defined? By war, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans said this is the place where you live, and that's it. And at some places, there were-- there were sort of fences. But on our-- in our side, there were none. Because opposite us, a lot of the Christians had to move out of the ghetto. Opposite that were-- was a Christian blacksmith. And for some reason, he couldn't move. And people visited him. And as they visited him, there was a way for them into the ghetto where they brought us-- not by weight, but by cups, butter and other goodies, which they preferred that we bartered and exchange them for our belongings, for goods, something to wear, rather than for the money. But they took money too. And that's where I first learned the psychology of bartering. And she asked for a higher price than, of course, she expected to get. We offered a much lower price. And she pretended to go. But she knew we wouldn't let her go, and the answer of-- and that's-- that's how-- that's how you-- you learned the psychology of this type of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did the wall around the ghetto, or the demarcation of the ghetto, come suddenly, or was there something going on before that happened that thought it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- we were told. And we had to move all into one place. And from the little villages, they were put into-- also into the ghettos. The villages were cleared, and everything went into the ghettos. Now, Bochnia was a small town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the non-Jewish population around Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't say. We had no contact except we-- we said hello to the-- the person-- to the blacksmith opposite. Otherwise, we had no--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't see much anti-Jewish discrimination with the locals, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: From what I've heard, there were-- there was. Personally, I haven't experienced--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't experience it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. As yet, there were no rewards for catching Jews. This comes later when we-- when there were rewards for catching Jews outside-- outside the ghettos, or those who possibly went on false papers as Aryans or non-Jews. If somebody-- some Poles found them and gave them away, they were given a kilo of sugar-- sugar, or some vodka, which was enough for them to betray us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very crowded in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how much more crowded can it get? Six people in-- in our room. But soon there was another-- the people of that bungalow had to give up another room. Another little family came there, two adults and a girl about two years older than myself. And well, this was quite a full house then. Now, when the ghettos were sold, you know, assigned, the rumors started flying that there were-- the Germans were sending special Einsatzgruppen, special groups, and they roamed from ghetto to ghetto and shot people at random. Now, there were rumors of 50 dead there, and 50 there. And a week or two later, it came up-- you know, it got up and up and up. 100, 150, 200. The end was 300. But apparently-- now, the youngsters of this ghetto, we met from time to time in groups. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Unfortunately, we couldn't organize resistance because we had no weapons, and nobody to really lead us. But we were talking about it, and regretting it. And we were calculating if they shoot as many as they do, if the war would-- would last 10 years, there would-- 10% of us would be killed. Little did we know there was less than 10% that survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the war didn't last for 10 years, as you know. Now, at one stage, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was asked to work for the Germans in an ammunition camp. And I got out of it by pretending I had tuberculosis. I knew all the symptoms. I went to a Polish doctor and got a certificate. But when the time came that they-- we heard that there were transports to the east, which the Germans called resettlement-- now I forgot the-- the German word for it. Doesn't really matter now. Resettlement.

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN]. It doesn't matter. And we didn't trust it. But what should we do? Now, at that time, when the time came nearer-- of course, the Einsatzgruppen, for one reason or another, maybe-- they didn't come to Bochnia. And in January, 1942-- but that is, of course, I only learned later.

speaker: interviewee
text: The famous or rather infamous Wannsee or German Wannsee Conference where 15 people decided [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fate of the Jews, the final solution. And it wasn't to be by Einsatzgruppen anymore. It was to build extermination camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we didn't know that that time, of course. But we didn't trust it. So I accepted-- suddenly accepted the work in the ammunition camp. And shortly before-- on the 24th-- on the 24th of August was the first transport from Bochnia. On the 22nd, I managed to take my father with me. And also, we discussed a hiding place. Now, we had no tools. We had no material. So in my book-- I'm not sure if I should show it to you. I show it to you in a minute. In my book, I have the sketch of the-- illustrated the hiding place where Mother and sister stayed overnight. It was the triangular part above the entrance porch, which you could reach from the attic. It was a bungalow. And the entrance to which we had a-- a piece of cardboard and put it so nobody can see that. Now, somebody walking up the stairs looking into the attic, he finds it empty. A thorough judge-- as-- a-- a thorough search would, of course, not stop anybody to finding the hiding place. Now, before that, I have to sort of-- for a certain reason which will be apparent later, I have to switch back again. Where I worked, we could find enough pieces of wood which I took back home in order not to suffer what we suffered the--the winter before, a shortage of material for-- for-- for a stove and warmth. And every time I came home, I was sort of proud that I could pad the heap behind the house. At one time, I-- my bundle was much too big. I couldn't get into the train. And I had to sit the ledge of the-- the edge of the wagon with the bundle over the buffers, and my feet against them, holding my self and balancing the way I wouldn't want my children to imitate me. And suddenly, one of the branches got loose and danced on the buffer. It-- each time the rails met, there's a little hum. Now they're trying to do away with it in-- in-- and-- sort of in Germany, it was a long time ago. Now they do it away with it as well. You don't-- you don't-- it's quite smooth now. If you go certain stretches by rail, other stretches are still not-- you know, still under with those connecting rails. Anyway, these hums cause the wood to jump. And it was dancing to the left. And I thought it's going to fall and perhaps derail the train. It started jumping again to the right. And a few times-- and each time I started sweating. You know, it if derails the train, will it kill us all. I managed at one stage when it was rather in-- in the middle to put my foot against it. But other pieces which I had loosened when I took away my foot managed to fall free, but harmlessly under the train. Now, this little episode will come later back into our mind. And anyway, this-- of course-- wood for the winter, was never to be enjoyed by us. But who it would be enjoyed, I don't know. My father was working with me already in the ammunition camp of-- of Klaj. And on the 24th there was a transport. And we were told not to come back home anymore at-- in evening. We-- they prepared barracks for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: To ammunition camp of Klaj, which was about 10 kilometers away by train. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were commuting there every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was commuting for quite a few weeks, for a few weeks. And then last couple of days, we had to stay there. Maybe they were told about the transport. I'm not sure. But they prepared barracks. The-- the ghettos will be emptied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there. We were-- on a-- said goodbye to my mother and sister. And because I told-- I told my story so often, I'm not going to break down now. Because this is one of the saddest moments. We did not hug unduly, as if not to-- nobody should sort of show that it-- in our heart, we might think it might be forever. But you had a thought at that time that it might be forever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one fears what one hears, what happens, the shootings. And when we were told resettlement, we didn't know what to believe. But we had no alternative other than to believe it, or to hope for the best. Now, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we ask Mother and sister to promise to go into the-- the hiding place. They did promise. And on the 24th, it was our hope that they would be in the hiding place when the transport in Bochnia took place.
